title: Macramé bracelet
subtitle: An adjustable square-knot bracelet on 2 mm waxed polyester or 3 mm cotton cord
excerpt: Four cords tied in a square-knot body of about 30 cm, finished with a sliding-closure mount so the bracelet adjusts to any wrist size. The cord-cut formula, the sliding-knot assembly, and the fringe finish are all covered step by step.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from the mid-20th-century macramé revival and sailors' ropework tradition. The square knot is the structural core of most knotted cord jewellery. Sliding-closure mounts using two opposing square knots are a standard macramé bracelet finish across the guild tradition. The square knot itself is documented in Graumont and Hensel, Encyclopedia of Knots and Fancy Rope Work (1939), Cornell Maritime Press.

glossaryTerms.definition: In macramé, the cord that actively forms the knot by moving over, under, or around the other cords. In a square knot the two outer cords are the working cords and the two inner cords are the filler cords, which remain stationary. Working cords are consumed faster than filler cords and should be cut longer when the pattern calls for unequal cord lengths.

The stationary inner cord or cords that the working cords knot around. In a square knot two filler cords run down the centre of each knot group, providing the structure the knot grips. Filler cords can be cut shorter than working cords because they are consumed slowly.

A symmetrical four-cord knot built from two opposing half-knots. The left working cord crosses over the filler cords and the right working cord follows under; the move is then reversed to complete the square. Each completed square knot sits flat, even, and will not spiral or twist when the tension is correct.

A bracelet fastening made from two square knots tied on a short separate length of cord looped around both bracelet tails. The two square knots grip the bracelet cords and slide along them, allowing the wearer to open and close the bracelet without a clasp. Pulling both tails apart opens the loop; pushing both knots toward each other closes it.

content.text: This bracelet is made from four cords: two outer
working cords
and two inner
filler cords
, all knotted in
square knots
along a body of about 30 cm, then finished with a
sliding closure

so the bracelet fits any wrist without a clasp. Use 2 mm waxed polyester cord for a firm, water-resistant bracelet that holds its shape under daily wear, or 3 mm cotton macramé cord for a softer, more textile look. The cord-cut formula: working cords at four times the finished body length, filler cords at the finished body length plus 30 cm for the closure ends. For a 30 cm body: cut the two working cords at 120 cm each, and cut the two filler cords at 60 cm each.
What you need

items.name: 2 mm waxed polyester cord or 3 mm cotton macramé cord
items.substitution: Waxed polyester for a water-resistant outdoor or everyday bracelet; cotton for a softer finish

content.text: The knots, step by step

The bracelet body uses one knot type. The square knot is tied with the two outer working cords around the two stationary inner filler cords. Tying the same move in the same order produces a flat column of square knots. Alternating which cord leads (left first, then right first) also makes a square knot column; the distinction matters only when you want to produce the spiral half-hitch variation instead.

definition.caption: Square knot: left working cord over fillers, right under fillers and up through the loop; reverse to complete the square.

content.text: The sliding closure uses the same square knot tied on a short separate cord looped around both bracelet tails. Two square knots tied around both tails create a gripping mount that slides when pushed but stays in place under tension.
Method
Step 1: Cut the cords to length

Cut two working cords at 120 cm each and two filler cords at 60 cm each. Use the cord-measuring tape to mark each length before cutting. Cut cleanly with sharp scissors; a clean cut keeps the cord end from fraying during knotting. If you are using 3 mm cotton cord, seal each cut end briefly with a lighter flame held 5 mm from the end, just long enough to melt the outer twist without burning the fibres. Waxed polyester does not need sealing.
Step 2: Set up the starting tie point

Lay the two filler cords side by side on the macramé board. Lay one working cord across both filler cords at the 10 cm mark from one end of the working cord, so the two filler cords cross the working cord at their midpoint. Lay the second working cord directly beside the first. Pin all four cords to the board with T-pins at the top so the cords hang straight and the four ends are clear below.
The short upper end of each working cord becomes one of the two bracelet tails that the sliding closure will grip. The long lower portion of each working cord is the knotting end.
Step 3: Knot the body

Hold the two filler cords taut with your non-dominant hand or pin their lower ends to the board. With the left working cord, cross it over both filler cords and under the right working cord, forming a loop on the right side. Now take the right working cord under both filler cords and up through the loop formed by the left cord. Pull both working cords outward and downward firmly so the first half-knot seats against the top pin.

Reverse the move to complete the square: take the right working cord over both filler cords and under the left working cord. Take the left working cord under both filler cords and up through the new loop. Pull both working cords outward evenly. The knot should sit flat and square; if it skews to one side, the tension was unequal on the pull. Re-seat the knot by easing the slacker side out and pulling firmly again.

Continue tying square knots closely butted together, working downward, until the knotted column measures 30 cm. A standard adult wrist needs roughly 15 to 17 cm of body for a comfortable fit. The sliding closure adds adjustability, so a 30 cm body fits a narrow wrist as well as a wide one. Keep the knots evenly tensioned throughout. Each knot pulled to the same firmness produces a column of equal texture. Knots pulled harder or softer read as lumps in the finished piece.
Step 4: Finish the lower end

At the end of the 30 cm body, tie one overhand knot in all four cords together close against the last square knot to hold the column in place while you build the sliding closure. Trim the four cord ends to 4 cm below the overhand knot for the lower fringe. These lower fringe ends will be visible when the bracelet is worn open at its widest.
Step 5: Build the sliding closure

Cut a short separate closure cord at 30 cm. Form the bracelet into a loop by bringing the lower fringe end up alongside the upper bracelet tails so that the top four ends and the lower four ends lie parallel, pointing in opposite directions. The two bracelet tails from the top of the body will slide through the closure; the two lower ends will also slide.

Lay the 30 cm closure cord across the parallel bundle of upper tails and lower tails. Using the closure cord as the working cord, tie two square knots around the bundle, keeping the knots close together and pulling firmly. The two ends of the closure cord become a short fringe. Trim these ends to 2 cm and seal with a lighter flame if using polyester. Pull both bracelet tails gently to check the closure slides smoothly; push the two square-knot mount cords toward each other to close the bracelet, and pull the tails apart to open it.
Variations

Tying the first half-knot the same way every time rather than reversing it produces a half-hitch spiral instead of a flat square column. The spiral rotates naturally as the cord is consumed; let it turn rather than fighting it. Aim for the same spacing per half-knot as per full square knot.

Adding a small bead to one filler cord before starting the body places the bead as a central decorative feature. Push the bead to the midpoint of the filler cord and tie the first square knot above it so the bead is framed between knots. Two or three spaced beads along the filler cord produce an evenly spaced bead station at each knot gap.

Two working cords in different colours, one on each side, produce a two-tone square knot column where each half-knot in the square shows a different colour on the face. This works best with 2 mm cord where both working cords are the same diameter.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The knot column spirals or twists instead of lying flat
items.cause: The half-knot direction was reversed partway through the body, or the first half of each square knot was not always started from the same side
items.fix: Establish a consistent starting side at the first knot and keep the same left-over-first sequence throughout. If the twist has already set in, undo back to the last flat knot and re-knot from there. Mark the left working cord with a small piece of tape at the start to keep the side clear.

items.problem: The working cords run out before the body reaches 30 cm
items.cause: The working cords were cut too short, or the knots were tied with more cord consumed per knot than the formula allows
items.fix: For tightly butted knots, four times the finished length is a minimum. Knots tied with wider spacing consume less cord per knot; closely butted knots with firm tension consume more. Cut working cords at 5 times the finished body length for the first attempt to build in a safe margin.

items.problem: The sliding closure grips too tightly and will not slide
items.cause: The two square knots of the closure were pulled very firmly, or the closure cord is the same diameter as the bracelet cords and the combined bundle is too stiff
items.fix: Loosen the closure square knots slightly by easing the working ends back. If the cord diameter is causing friction, try a thinner closure cord: a single strand of waxed thread or a lighter-weight cord slides more freely around a thicker bundle.
